script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. Friday — here is your QQQM recap. The portfolio closed one point eight nine percent up on the session. A strong day anchored by outsized moves in semiconductors and AI-adjacent names, with one major legal headline landing after the close.

Apple has filed a federal lawsuit against OpenAI. The accusation: a coordinated campaign to steal confidential product designs and AI hardware trade secrets. OpenAI never responded to Apple's preliminary findings, which were shared back in February. That silence is now the centerpiece of a federal case.

No macro data of significance hit the tape on Friday. The broad market finished higher, led by technology and communication services, with the Nasdaq Composite advancing roughly one point seven percent on the session. Semiconductors carried the index, with multiple names in this portfolio posting gains well above the index average.

The headline of Friday's session, setting aside the after-hours legal filing, belongs to Advanced Micro Devices. Shares closed at five hundred fifty-seven dollars and eighty-nine cents, up seven point eight two percent — the strongest single-session gain among primary holdings. That kind of move does not happen in a vacuum. The catalyst sits squarely in the AI accelerator market, where Advanced Micro Devices continues to close the competitive gap with Nvidia. Analyst sentiment has been building around the company's MI-series GPU roadmap, and portfolio positioning reflects the conviction that this is a two-horse race in the data center accelerator space. At three point eight three percent of the portfolio, Advanced Micro Devices punched well above its weight on Friday, contributing meaningfully to the session's overall gain. The stock is now printing at levels that demand attention heading into earnings season. What analysts are watching closely: whether management can deliver forward guidance on MI-series shipment volumes that supports the valuation the market is assigning. Any shortfall there would be a risk event. But Friday's price action suggests the market is not waiting for confirmation — it is already pricing in the bull case.

Turning to Nvidia, which closed at two hundred ten dollars and ninety-six cents, up three point three five percent on the session. Nvidia remains the largest position in this portfolio at seven point six percent, and Friday's move added meaningfully to the week's gains. No company-specific news drove the move directly. Semiconductor sentiment was broadly positive, and Nvidia continues to function as the bellwether for AI infrastructure spending. The stock to watch into next week is whether institutional flows continue to favor Nvidia at current levels or rotate toward names with more catch-up potential.

Apple closed at three hundred fifteen dollars and thirty-two cents, up zero point six two percent on the session. The modest gain belies a significant after-hours development. Apple filed a federal lawsuit against OpenAI, alleging theft of trade secrets related to AI hardware, including product designs and, notably, a laptop. According to reporting from multiple sources, Apple notified OpenAI of its preliminary findings in February and received no response. The stock was unmoved in after-hours trading following the filing. Investors appear to be treating this as a legal dispute rather than a fundamental business threat — for now.

Alphabet closed at three hundred fifty-seven dollars and eighteen cents, down one point three one percent on the session. The decline stands out given the broader market strength. No direct company catalyst explained the pullback. One piece of relevant context: reporting circulated Friday noting that broader index funds outperformed Alphabet's standalone return meaningfully over the past year, raising questions about whether the stock's valuation fully captures its cloud growth trajectory. The next major catalyst for Alphabet remains its earnings report, where cloud revenue growth will be the number that matters most.

Micron Technology closed at nine hundred seventy-nine dollars and thirty cents, up three point two one percent on the session. Memory cycle dynamics remain the central narrative here. Reporting from finance.yahoo.com flagged that SanDisk is trading as though the memory cycle has bottomed and been rebuilt — management commentary that goes beyond a traditional cyclical recovery and suggests a structural improvement in the business model. Micron, as the dominant domestic DRAM and NAND player, trades on similar dynamics. Watch for any inventory or pricing commentary out of the supply chain next week.

Microsoft closed at three hundred eighty-five dollars and ten cents, up zero point four six percent. A quiet session for Microsoft relative to the broader tech rally. The stock continues to consolidate around its current range. AI infrastructure spending from enterprise clients remains the primary long-term driver. Earnings season will test whether Azure growth is accelerating or plateauing.

Over at Amazon, shares closed at two hundred forty-five dollars and thirty-four cents, up zero point seven one percent. Amazon was flagged by HSBC as one of ten stocks to watch heading into earnings season, according to reporting from finance.yahoo.com. AWS growth and advertising revenue will be the two metrics that move the stock on its report date. No new operational news on Friday.

Tesla closed at four hundred seven dollars and seventy-six cents, up three point four eight percent on the session. Reporting out of finance.yahoo.com noted that New Jersey is considering legislation that would ban self-driving vehicles that do not use radar and lidar — a direct challenge to Tesla's camera-only autonomous system. Separately, Tesla is gaining market share in China. The regulatory overhang in New Jersey is a risk to watch, but China market share momentum is a meaningful offset. The stock's robotaxi narrative remains sensitive to any new state-level restrictions.

Intel closed at one hundred nine dollars and eighty-four cents, down zero point three six percent. A modest decline on a strong day for semiconductors broadly. Intel continues to lag its peers in the AI accelerator buildout. No new catalysts on Friday. The upcoming earnings report will be closely watched for any update on the foundry business timeline and whether the turnaround narrative is gaining traction with institutional holders.

Three data points to watch in the next forty-eight hours. First, any regulatory response from OpenAI or its counsel regarding Apple's federal lawsuit — statements from either party this weekend could set the tone for the stock when markets reopen. Second, earnings calendar confirmation for Micron Technology and Microsoft, both of which are approaching their reporting windows — exact dates have not yet been formally locked in for all institutional calendars, making any pre-announcement guidance material. Third, semiconductor supply chain commentary from Asia — Taiwan Semiconductor and related manufacturers often release informal channel checks over the weekend that move names like Nvidia, Micron, and Advanced Micro Devices at Monday's open.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. Watch the Apple and OpenAI legal situation closely — what happens over the weekend in terms of official responses could define the narrative heading into the new trading week.
